coredata.dc:description: This study introduces a novel nanocomposite fluorescent probe (N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP) for the sensitive and selective detection of meclofenamic acid, a non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug, in dairy products. The probe combines nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots, citrate-functionalized hydroxyapatite, and a molecularly imprinted polymer, achieving a detection limit of 0.03 μg L−1 and recoveries of 93.2–99.9 % with RSDs below 3.1 %. The sensor offers superior sensitivity and reduced analysis time compared to chromatographic methods. Furthermore, its compatibility with smartphone-based detection systems highlights its potential for rapid on-site food safety monitoring.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The synthesis strategy for the proposed N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP probe (A) and the procedure for determining meclofenamic acid in real samples with the proposed probe (B).

A photograph of N-GQDs under UV light in a black box (A); a TEM image of N-GQDs (B); a particle size distribution histogram of N-GQDs (C); SEM images of Cit-HA (D) and N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP (E); a particle size distribution histogram of N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP (F); the N2 adsorption and desorption curves of Cit-HA (G) and the TGA curve of N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP (H).

The quenching of the fluorescence of the N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP probe by meclofenamic acid stopped increasing at 24 min (A). The charts show the effects on the sensitivity of the proposed probe of dispersion solution pH (B), template to monomer to cross-linker mole ratio (C) and Cit-HA loading (D).

The sensitivity of different sensing probes for the detection of meclofenamic acid (A). UV–vis absorption spectra of meclofenamic acid, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of the N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP probe (B).

The fluorescence emissions of the N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP (A) and N-GQDs@Cit-HA@NIP (B) probes were measured in various concentrations of meclofenamic acid and linear calibration curves (C) were plotted: N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP (blue dot line) and N-GQDs@Cit-HA@NIP (red dot line). The photograph shows the N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP probe in the absence (left) and presence (right) of meclofenamic acid at 50.0 μg L−1 (D). The chart (E) shows the selectivity of the N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP and N-GQDs@Cit-HA@NIP probes toward meclofenamic acid and structural analogues (mefenamic acid, diclofenac, diflunisal and flurbiprofen). Chart (F) shows the results of a competitive study involving both probes in the presence of various ratios of meclofenamic acid and mefenamic acid. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The setup and use of the smartphone-integrated fluorescence sensing system for the determination of meclofenamic acid is illustrated (A). The calibration curve for the determination of meclofenamic acid from 5.0 to 50.0 μg L−1 was constructed using the smartphone-assisted method (B). The linear relationship between fluorescence quenching (F0/F) and meclofenamic acid concentration obtained with the smartphone-assisted method (Imean) was close to the linear relationship obtained with the fluorescence spectrophotometer (C).

HPLC chromatograms of milk samples spiked with meclofenamic acid at concentrations of 0.0, 5.0, 10.0, 15.0 and 20.0 μg L−1 (A). Comparison of the results of analysis with the proposed fluorescent sensor using the N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP probe and HPLC (B).
The determination of meclofenamic acid in milk, yogurt and cheese samples by spectrofluorometry using the proposed probe and by HPLC (n = 3).
A comparison of analytical methods for the determination of meclofenamic acid.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study introduces a novel nanocomposite fluorescent probe (N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP) for the sensitive and selective detection of meclofenamic acid, a non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug, in dairy products. The probe combines nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots, citrate-functionalized hydroxyapatite, and a molecularly imprinted polymer, achieving a detection limit of 0.03 μg L−1 and recoveries of 93.2–99.9 % with RSDs below 3.1 %. The sensor offers superior sensitivity and reduced analysis time compared to chromatographic methods. Furthermore, its compatibility with smartphone-based detection systems highlights its potential for rapid on-site food safety monitoring.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Meclofenamic acid is an anthranilic acid derivative used as a non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAIDs) in veterinary medicine for the treatment of pain, fever, inflammation and bovine respiratory diseases. The primary mechanism of action is the inhibition of cyclooxygenase, which is responsible for the synthesis of prostaglandins (Peng et al., 2013). However, the misuse of meclofenamic acid leaves residues in the food chain which can enter the human body, posing health risks such as inhibition of platelet aggregation, intestinal ulceration and gastrointestinal bleeding (Gallo et al., 2010). To protect consumer health, the European Union has set a maximum residue limit (MRL) for NSAIDs in milk of 0.1 μg kg−1 (Commission Regulation (EU), 2009). Consequently, food safety concerns demand reliable and accurate analytical methods of detecting meclofenamic acid in certain animal products.

Various methods of determining meclofenamic acid have been reported, including ultra-performance liquid chromatography (Peng et al., 2013), high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Gallo et al., 2010; Shaaban & Górecki, 2011), gas chromatography (Gumbi, Moodley, Birungi, & Ndungu, 2017), capillary electrophoresis (Pérez-Ruiz, Martınez-Lozano, Sanz, & Bravo, 1998) and spectrofluorometry (Muñoz de la Peña, Mora Díez, Bohoyo Gil, Olivieri, & Escandar, 2006). Chromatographic analysis is the method most often employed due to its good precision and high sensitivity. However, it is time-consuming and usually requires complicated sample preparation procedures and expensive instruments (Chaowana & Bunkoed, 2019). Spectrofluorometry is a simpler technique which is fast and inexpensive (Omar, Haredy, Saleh, Naggar, & Derayea, 2019), and by using quantum dots (QDs) as fluorescent probes, the sensitivity of the technique can be enhanced.

QDs have excellent optical properties and good photostability (Hasan et al., 2019). Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a carbon-based material, have broad applications as fluorescent probes. They can be easily synthesized by bottom-up methods such as pyrolysis (Li et al., 2021). Recent reports have demonstrated that doping with heteroatoms enriches the fluorescence properties of GQDs through the introduction of additional functional groups that improve the determination of target analytes (Ghafarloo, Emamali Sabzi, Samadi, & Hamishehkar, 2020). GQDs have been doped with nitrogen (N) (Ramachandran & Karunakaran Yesodha, 2019), boron (B) (Ma et al., 2020) and sulfur (S) (Kadian & Manik, 2020), and co-doped with multiplex heteroatoms such as N-S (Safardoust-Hojaghan et al., 2019), BN (Liu et al., 2020) and SP (Wang et al., 2019). N is the favored choice for doping GQDs since it has a similar atomic size to C and provides five valence electrons that can form strong covalent CN bonds with graphene (Jin, Wang, Yan, Zhang, & Zhong, 2019). The nitrogen doping of GQDs enhances their quantum yield, and the surface functional groups introduced allow N-GQDs to emit strong fluorescence (Kashani, Madrakian, & Afkhami, 2017).

The selectivity and specificity of fluorescent probes can be improved by integrating QDs with molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP). This approach has been used for the determination of tryptophan (Wu et al., 2020), thiabendazole (Kazemifard, Ensafi, & Rezaei, 2020) and tetrabromobisphenol A (Shao et al., 2023). These probes are easy to synthesize and have shown good stability and specificity (Kazemifard et al., 2020; Shao et al., 2023). MIP is normally synthesized by a polymerization process combining a template molecule (the target analyte) with a functional monomer and cross-linker to form a three-dimensional polymer network (Decompte, Lobaz, Monperrus, Deniau, & Save, 2020). After polymerization, the template molecule is removed from the polymer, leaving recognition sites that are closely matched in size and shape to the target molecule. The target analyte can then bind specifically to the imprinted polymer (Karrat & Amine, 2021). Furthermore, fluorescent probes can incorporate high-affinity materials to enhance the adsorption of the target molecule and reduce analysis time. Citrate-functionalized hydroxyapatite (Cit-HA) contains hydroxyl groups which can adsorb meclofenamic acid via hydrogen bonding. Cit-HA has also attracted attention because of its large surface area, biocompatibility and good stability (Bunkoed, Donkhampa, & Nurerk, 2020; Verma, Barick, Shetake, Pandey, & Hassan, 2016).

In this work, a highly sensitive and selective fluorescent probe for the determination of meclofenamic acid was fabricated by incorporating nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots and citrate-functionalized hydroxyapatite into molecularly imprinted polymer (N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP). The quantitative analysis of meclofenamic acid was based on the quenching of fluorescence that occurred when meclofenamic acid bound to the specific recognition sites on the surface MIP layer of the probe. The fluorescent sensor was applied to determine meclofenamic acid in milk, yogurt and cheese samples.

Meclofenamic acid was from Sigma-Aldrich (MO, USA). 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES, ≥98 %), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, ≥99 %) and formic acid were from Tokyo Chemical Industry Co. Ltd. (Tokyo, Japan). Phosphoric acid (H3PO4) was from J.T. Baker (USA). Tris(hydroxymethyl)-aminomethane (Tris-HMA) was from Sigma-Aldrich (Germany). Citric acid was purchased from KEMAUS (Western Australia). Calcium hydroxide (Ca(OH)2) was from Quality Reagent Chemical (New Zealand). Disodium hydrogen orthophosphate dodecahydrate (Na2HPO4·12H2O) was from Univar (Ajax Finechem, Australia). Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) was from Loba Chemie Pvt. Ltd. (Mumbai, India). Nitric acid (HNO3), ammonia (NH3, 25 %), ethanol, acetonitrile and methanol were obtained from RCI Labscan (Bangkok, Thailand).

Fluorescence intensity was measured using a Cary eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA). The functional groups of related materials were examined by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) (Bruker, Germany). Absorption spectra were measured with a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2600i, Shimadzu, Japan). The morphologies of related materials were observed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Talos F200i, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Czech Republic) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Czech Republic). The thermal stability of the nanocomposite probe was assessed by thermogravimetric analysis (PerkinElmer, USA). Surface areas were calculated using the Branauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method (ASAP2060, Micromeritics, USA). X-ray diffractometry (XRD, Bruker, Karlsruhe, Germany) was utilized to investigate the structure of N-GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: N-GQDs were synthesized following the method described in a previous work (Chumkaeo, Davis, & Bunkoed, 2024; Lin et al., 2015). Citric acid (2.00 g) and Tris-HMA (0.50 g) were placed in a round-bottom flask and heated at 225 °C for 10 min. The formation of N-GQDs was evidenced by the appearance of a solid orange product, that was allowed to cool to room temperature and then dispersed in 5.0 mL of deionized water. Subsequently, 1.0 M NaOH was added dropwise until the pH of the solution reached 7.0. The resulting N-GQDs solution was stored at 4 °C for further use.

The synthesis procedure of Cit-HA was adapted from previous reports (Bunkoed et al., 2020; Verma et al., 2016). Briefly, 1.60 g of Ca(OH)2 and 1.50 g of citric acid were dissolved in 100.0 mL of deionized water and added dropwise to 50.0 mL of 0.1 M H3PO4. The mixture solution was stirred for 1 h at 60 °C, adjusted to pH 11.0 with 1.0 M NaOH, and further stirred for 3 h. The resulting precipitate was filtered and washed three times with ethanol and deionized water. Finally, the synthesized Cit-HA was dried in an oven at 60 °C.

The N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP and N-GQDs@Cit-HA@NIP probes were dispersed in citrate-phosphate buffer solution at pH 5.0, which was the condition that produced the highest sensitivity. At room temperature, 300 μL of a dispersion of N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP or N-GQDs@Cit-HA@NIP were mixed with 100 μL of meclofenamic acid standard or sample solution, and incubated for 24 min. The mixture was then transferred to a quartz cell ( Fig. 1B). Fluorescence measurements were recorded using the fluorescence spectrophotometer with a slit width of 10 nm. The excitation wavelength was set at 330 nm and the emission wavelength ranged from 335 to 700 nm.

Milk, yogurt and cheese samples were pretreated before analysis with the developed N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP probe. The pretreatment of milk samples was based on a previous report (Orachorn & Bunkoed, 2019). To remove protein and fat, 15.0 mL of milk were centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 20 min. Subsequently, 15.0 mL of acetonitrile were added and the sample was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for a further 10 min. The resulting supernatant was filtered and evaporated to dryness at 60 °C. The dried extract was dissolved in 10.0 mL of methanol.

Yogurt samples were pretreated as described in a previous work (Chen et al., 2019). First, 1.00 g of yogurt was extracted with 20.0 mL of methanol with sonication for 20 min. Then, 10.0 mL of deionized water were added and the mixture was kept at 4 °C for 30 min. The mixture was then centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was filtered and then evaporated at 60 °C. The residue was dissolved in 10.0 mL of methanol.

The pretreatment of cheese samples was modified from a previous report (Kouti, Tsiasioti, Zacharis, & Tzanavaras, 2021). Briefly, 5.00 g of cheese were blended with 50.0 mL of HNO3 and homogenized. This mixture was sonicated for 20 min, chilled at 4 °C for 30 min, and centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane and evaporated at 60 °C. Finally, the dried extract was dissolved with 10.0 mL of methanol.

HPLC analysis was conducted using the Agilent 1100 series (Germany). Samples were separated in a Fortis C18 column (150 mm × 4.6 mm I.D., 5.0 μm). The mobile phase comprised 0.10 % v/v formic acid and methanol (15:85 v/v), flowed at 1.0 mL min−1. The injection volume was 20 μL and detection was by diode array at 220 nm.

The functional groups of related materials and the developed N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP probe were investigated by FTIR spectroscopy. In the spectra of HA and Cit-HA (Fig. S1A), the stretching and bending vibrations of OH, CaO and PO of the phosphate group appear at similar wavenumbers. In the spectrum of Cit-HA, the peak at 1616 cm−1 corresponds to the asymmetric stretching of CO of the carboxylic group of citric acid, which appears at 1728 cm−1 in the spectrum of citric acid. The shift observed in the spectrum of Cit-HA was attributed to the binding between citric acid and calcium atoms on the surface of HA through the chemisorption of carboxylate (citrate) groups (Verma et al., 2016). These results demonstrated the successful preparation of Cit-HA by the functionalization of HA with citrate ions.

The FTIR spectrum of N-GQDs (Fig. S1B) exhibits an absorption peak at 3410 cm−1 that corresponds to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH. The characteristic peaks at 1737, 1670 and 1578 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of CO, CC and the aromatic ring, respectively (Tamizharasan, Muralidharan, Abirami, Sankeetha, & Arulmozhi, 2024). The peaks at 1396 and 1217 cm−1 were ascribed to the stretching vibrations of CN and CO, respectively (Lin et al., 2015). In the spectrum of Cit-HA, the broad band around 3436 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of OH. The peaks at 1616 and 1275 cm−1 correspond to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching of CO and CO from the carboxylic group of citric acid, respectively. The characteristic peaks at 1077 and 1033 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibration of CaO and PO. The absorption peaks around 602 and 563 cm−1 indicate the bending of PO in the phosphate group. In the spectrum of N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP, the band at 3405 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of NH, representing the presence of the functional monomer (−NH2). Meanwhile, the specific broad peak at 1074 cm−1 was attributed to the asymmetric stretching of Si-O-Si. The peaks at 792 and 479 cm−1 indicate the stretching vibration of SiO and the presence of APTES and TEOS, providing evidence for the formation of the imprinted layer (Zhang et al., 2023). These results confirmed the successful synthesis of the N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP probe.

The optical properties of the synthesized N-GQDs were good and, in a dark box, the probe emitted a strong blue fluorescence under ultraviolet light ( Fig. 2A ). The TEM image of N-GQDs ( Fig. 2B) confirmed well dispersed uniform particles of approximately 6 nm on average (Fig. 2C). The high-resolution TEM image of N-GQDs reveals well-defined lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the hexagonal lattice structure of graphene (Algadi, Albargi, Umar, & Shkir, 2021).The surface morphologies of Cit-HA and N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP were examined by SEM. Cit-HA ( Fig. 2D) displayed an irregularly shaped structure whereas N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP demonstrated a well-defined spherical shape ( Fig. 2E) with an average particle size of about 430 nm ( Fig. 2F).

The surface area of the synthesized Cit-HA was evaluated from the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm (Fig. 2G). The BET method was employed to calculate the specific surface areas of Cit-HA and N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP, which were 135.6 and 4.1 m2 g−1, respectively. The surface area of N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP was reduced by the specific recognition sites in the imprinted layer but the recognition sites also increase the specificity of the sensor to the target analyte, resulting in more accurate measurements. Although the reduced surface area lowers the adsorption capacity of the sensor, trace amounts of meclofenamic acid residues in food can still be measured.

XRD analysis was employed to examine the structure of N-GQDs. The XRD pattern of N-GQDs revealed a broad peak at 2θ of 18.5° that was associated with the (002) plane of the graphitic structure (Fig. S2).

XPS analysis was conducted to examine the composition and surface functional groups of the N-GQDs. The spectra revealed three prominent peaks at 285.0, 400.0, and 531.0 eV, corresponding to C 1 s, O 1 s, and N 1 s, respectively. The atomic percentages of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen were 64.30 %, 3.35 %, and 32.36 %, respectively, indicating that these elements are the major components in the N-GQDs (Fig. S3A). In the C 1 s spectrum (Fig. S3B), five peaks were observed at 283.968, 285.000, 286.049, 287.186, and 288.446 eV, which are associated with the following functional groups: C=C/C–C, CN, C–OH, CO, and O − C=O. The N 1 s spectrum (Fig. S3C) presented three peaks at 398.525, 399.826, and 400.744 eV, representing pyridinic, pyrrolic, and graphitic nitrogen structures, respectively. Five peaks could be seen in the O 1 s spectrum (Fig. S3D). They were at 530.133, 531.273, 532.162, 532.933, and 534.140 eV, which are C–OH, CO, C–O–C, O–C=O, and H₂O. These XPS results confirm that carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen are the primary elements in the N-GQDs, with various surface functional groups containing oxygen and nitrogen (Chumkaeo et al., 2024).

The maximum absorption wavelength of N-GQDs was observed at 329 nm, while the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths were recorded at 330 nm and 410 nm, respectively. Notably, the fluorescence emission spectrum exhibited excitation independence (Fig. S4), indicating that the emission peak remained unchanged at different excitation wavelengths and highlighting the uniform surface structure of the N-GQDs.

The thermal stability of the N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP probe was investigated by TGA analysis (Fig. 2H). The weight loss observed up to 100 °C was due to the loss of physically adsorbed water. The weight loss observed between 200 °C and 400 °C, was caused by the decomposition of the imprinted polymer layer (Zhang et al., 2023). The weight loss observed above 400 °C was attributed to the elimination and degradation of chemisorbed citrate ions from Cit-HA nanoparticles (Verma et al., 2016). The study results indicated that the synthesized N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP was stable up to 200 °C and could therefore be applied for the analysis of meclofenamic acid at room temperature (25 °C). The analysis of real samples was performed at room temperature for convenience.

The fluorescence emission of the fabricated N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP probe peaked at 410 nm. The fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP before the removal of the template molecule was relatively weak. After the template molecule was removed, the fluorescence intensity of the probe was restored to nearly the same level as that of the non-imprinted probe (Fig. S5). These results confirmed that the template molecule was completely removed from the N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP probe.

The quantum yields (Φ) of N-GQDs and N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP were measured by comparing the integrated fluorescence emissions and the absorbance value using quinine as the standard reference (Φ = 54 %). This reference method is commonly employed for quantum yield evaluation. Φ was calculated using the following equation:
Φ X
=
Φ ST
Grad X
/
Grad ST
η 2
X
/
η 2
ST
where the subscripts X and ST represent the target substance (N-GQDs or N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP) and the reference quinine, respectively. Grad denotes the gradient of the integrated fluorescence emission of X or ST plotted against their absorbances: η is the refractive index of the solvent (water = 1.33) (Han et al., 2023). The Φ values of N-GQDs and N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP were 72.6 and 45.3, respectively. The fluorescence emission of N-GQDs@MIP was partially reduced compared to the emission of N-GQDs. The decrease in fluorescence can be attributed to an electric field generated by a single charge near the QDs surface, originating from SiO groups that caused surface defects (Liu, Fang, & Wang, 2014; Montaseri & Forbes, 2018). Consequently, the quantum yield of molecularly imprinted polymer was reduced. Nevertheless, N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP still demonstrated excellent fluorescence performance.

To accurately determine meclofenamic acid with the highest sensitivity in the shortest analysis time, the experimental parameters affecting fluorescence quenching were optimized. The optimized parameters included incubation time, dispersion solution pH, the mole ratio of template to monomer to cross-linker, and the amount of Cit-HA used in the fabrication of the probe. To determine the optimal condition, a calibration curve was plotted between quenching efficiency (F0/F) and meclofenamic acid concentration (from 0.10 to 50.0 μg L−1). The sensitivity of response in each condition was evaluated from the slope of the curve. The highest sensitivity indicated the optimal condition.

The F0/F of the N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP probe was studied from 2 to 30 min. F0/F increased with time to 24 min and then remained almost unchanged, indicating that the binding of meclofenamic acid to the imprinted polymer was complete (Fig. 3A). Therefore, 24 min was the optimal incubation time between meclofenamic acid and the probe.

The pH value of the dispersion solution potentially impacted the binding between the probe and meclofenamic acid. The effect of pH was tested by dispersing the probe in citrate-phosphate buffer at pH 4.0 to 7.0. The sensitivity of the N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP probe was found to be highest at pH 5.0 (Fig. 3B). At pH levels lower than 5.0, amino groups of the recognition cavities of the probe were protonated, leading to reduced hydrogen bonding between the recognition cavities and meclofenamic acid. In the alkaline condition, the ionization of silica in the imprinted layer damaged the structure of the recognition cavities. Thus, a dispersion pH of 5.0 was selected for meclofenamic acid detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mole ratio of template to monomer and cross-linker
ce:section.ce:para: The ratio of template (meclofenamic acid) to functional monomer (APTES) to cross-linker (TEOS) is crucial for the correct formation of recognition sites in the MIP layer. The mole ratio of template to monomer of 1:8 provided the highest sensitivity (Fig. 3C). Lower proportions of monomer produced fewer available functional groups (-NH2) to bind with the template, reducing the number of specific recognition sites formed. Sensitivity was therefore low. On the other hand, an excess of monomer led to self-condensation during synthesis. The formation of recognition sites was inhibited and sensitivity was therefore low. The mole ratio of template to cross-linker of 1:35 produced the best sensitivity. Insufficient cross-linker produced a polymer network with fewer crosslinks, leading to a weaker structure that affected the formation of imprinted cavities. Conversely, an excess of cross-linker increased the thickness of the imprinted layer encapsulating the fluorescence source (Pan et al., 2024) and the interaction between monomer and template molecule was hindered. Hence, the mole ratio of meclofenamic acid to APTES to TEOS of 1:8:35 was selected for the fabrication of the nanocomposite N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP probe.

The amount of Cit-HA included in the N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP probe was varied from 7.0 to 10.0 mg. The results indicated that 9.0 mg of Cit-HA provided the highest sensitivity (Fig. 3D). A Cit-HA loading of 10.0 mg could disrupt the creation of recognition cavities and the sensitivity of the probe was lower. At Cit-HA loadings of 7.0 and 8.0 mg, affinity binding between the probe and the analyte was not optimal and sensitivity was low. As a result, 9.0 mg of Cit-HA was used for the fabrication of the probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of different sensing probes and the quenching mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: The sensitivity and detection limits of different nanocomposite probes were evaluated for the determination of meclofenamic acid. The probes were N-GQDs@NIP, N-GQDs@MIP, N-GQDs@Cit-HA@NIP and N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP. The N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP sensing probe provided the highest sensitivity (Fig. 4A), which was attributed to the presence of specific recognition sites and Cit-HA, which can adsorb meclofenamic acid via hydrogen bonding. The N-GQDs@NIP probe exhibited the lowest sensitivity toward meclofenamic acid due to the absence of both specific imprinted cavities and Cit-HA. The detection limits of N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP, N-GQDs@MIP, N-GQDs@Cit-HA@NIP and N-GQDs@NIP were 0.03, 0.08, 0.17 and 0.34 μg L−1, respectively.

The fluorescence quenching of the nanocomposite N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP probe by meclofenamic acid can be described using the Stern-Volmer equation (Laddha, Yadav, Sharma, Agarwal, & Gupta, 2023; Xie, Zhang, Wu, Xiong, & Hao, 2024), F0/F = 1 + KSV[C], in which F0 and F are the fluorescence emission in the absence and presence of the quencher (meclofenamic acid), respectively. KSV is the quenching constant and [C] is the concentration of meclofenamic acid. The fluorescence intensity of the N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP was quenched when meclofenamic acid bound with recognition cavities of the probe. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of meclofenamic acid coincided with the fluorescence excitation spectrum of the N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP probe (Fig. 4B), indicating that meclofenamic acid quenched probe fluorescence via the inner filter effect (Laddha et al., 2023).

The analytical performances of the nanocomposite N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP and N-GQDs@Cit-HA@NIP probes were investigated under optimal conditions. The fluorescence emission spectra (Fig. 5A and B) showed that the fluorescence intensity of the N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP probe decreased more with increments of meclofenamic acid concentration than the fluorescence intensity of the N-GQDs@Cit-HA@NIP probe. Consequently, the N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP probe displayed much higher sensitivity toward meclofenamic acid than the N-GQDs@Cit-HA@NIP probe. The developed nanocomposite probe exhibited linearity in the range of 0.10 to 50.0 μg L−1 (Fig. 5C). The linear equations for the N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP and N-GQDs@Cit-HA@NIP probes were y = (0.0236 ± 0.0010)x + (1.1535 ± 0.0236) and y = (0.0038 ± 0.0002)x + (1.0288 ± 0.0045), respectively. The coefficients of determination (R2) were greater than 0.99. The limit of detection (LOD) and quantification (LOQ) were calculated based on 3σ/Slope and 10σ/Slope, where σ represented the standard deviation of blank measurement (Abedeen, Sharma, Kushwaha, & Gupta, 2025). The LOD and LOQ were 0.03 μg L−1 and 0.10 μg L−1, respectively. The imprinting factor (IF) was evaluated to assess the specificity of the N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP toward meclofenamic acid. The IF was calculated based on the ratio of the sensitivity of N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP to N-GQDs@Cit-HA@NIP. The N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP probe exhibited an IF of 6.2, which implied that large numbers of specific binding sites were produced on the surface of the probe. A photograph showing the N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP probe in the absence and presence of meclofenamic acid is shown in Fig. 5D.

The selectivity of the N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP probe was investigated by applying the nanocomposite probe to determine analogue compounds of meclofenamic acid, including mefenamic acid, diclofenac, diflunisal and flurbiprofen. The probe displayed much higher sensitivity for the determination of meclofenamic acid than for its analogue structures (Fig. 5E) due to the presence of specific binding sites on the probe which matched the size, shape and functional groups of meclofenamic acid. The response of the N-GQDs@Cit-HA@NIP probe was lower due to the absence of specific binding sites in the non-imprinted polymer. The results indicated that the nanocomposite N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP probe exhibited good selectivity toward meclofenamic acid.

The competitive binding ability of the nanocomposite N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP probe was also assessed by mixing mefenamic acid and meclofenamic acid solutions at various concentration ratios (Cmefenamic acid/Cmeclofenamic acid). Sensitivity remained unchanged ( Fig. 5F), confirming the selectivity and specificity of the probe. These results demonstrated that the probe could effectively determine meclofenamic acid in the complex matrices of food samples. Furthermore, other interferences that may be encountered in food samples, such as salt, have demonstrated minimal impact on the optical properties of quantum dots (Ni et al., 2024).

The stability of the fabricated probe was assessed by measuring its fluorescence intensity every 30 min for 6 h in a buffer solution at pH 5.0. The fluorescence intensity remained nearly unchanged over 360 min (Fig. S6 A), which is sufficient time to determine actual samples using spectrofluorometry. The results demonstrated that the N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP probe possessed good stability. To evaluate the reproducibility of the N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP probe, six different probes were fabricated under identical conditions at different times and were used to detect meclofenamic acid. The probe exhibited excellent reproducibility with an RSD of 1.9 % (Fig. S6B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-integrated fluorescence sensing device for onsite applications
ce:section.ce:para: The N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP probe was integrated with a smartphone-assisted system designed for the on-site determination of meclofenamic acid. The sensor was placed in a dark enclosure measuring 220 × 270 × 145 mm. A 365 nm UV lamp (3 W) was the excitation source to produce fluorescence. Power was supplied by a portable power bank. Samples were secured between the UV lamp and the aperture at the front of the dark box. A 400 nm filter was positioned over the aperture at the front of the dark box to eliminate background light interference during fluorescence measurement. Fluorescence emission measurements and data were collected using a smartphone positioned adjacent to the aperture at the front of the dark box. The N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP probe was mixed with various concentrations of meclofenamic acid, placed in the dark box and incubated for 24 min. A visible fluorescence change was observed at 365nm. Fluorescent color images were captured with a smartphone and processed into RGB values using the Color Picker application (Yadav et al., 2024). The RGB values were then converted into grayscale using the formula: I = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B (Hu et al., 2024). The blue fluorescence was gradually quenched with increments of meclofenamic acid concentration from 5.0- to 50.0 μg L−1 (Fig. 6A ). The corresponding linear equation was Imean = (0.0128 ± 0.0005)Cmeclofenamic acid + (0.1466 ± 0.0138) with an R2 of 0.9939 (Fig. 6B), where the mean grayscale value Imean = (Iblank - Itest)/Iblank. The LOD was 1.5 μg L−1, determined based on 3σ/Slope. The linear relationship closely resembled the linear relationship derived from fluorescence measurements obtained with the fluorescence spectrophotometer (Fig. 6C). The results indicated that the smartphone-integrated fluorescence sensing method could effectively determine meclofenamic acid.

The fluorescent sensor was utilized with the fluorescence spectrophotometer to determine meclofenamic acid in milk, yogurt and cheese samples. The accuracy of the developed sensor was assessed by spiking various concentrations of meclofenamic acid (5.0, 10.0, 15.0 and 20.0 μg L−1 or μg kg−1) in milk, yogurt and cheese samples. As summarized in Table 1 , recoveries ranged from 93.2 to 99.9 % with an RSD lower than 3.1 %. Meclofenamic acid in the real samples was also determined by HPLC, when recoveries were in the range of 88.2 to 99.2 % with an RSD below 4.4 %. Typical HPLC chromatograms of a real milk sample and samples spiked with meclofenamic acid at different concentrations are shown in Fig. 7A. The results of analysis with the proposed sensor were highly consistent with those of analysis with HPLC with an R2 of 0.9973 (Fig. 7B). The developed fluorescent sensor showed good accuracy and reliability for the quantitative analysis of meclofenamic acid in real samples. Furthermore, the performance of the developed fluorescent sensor was compared with those of previously reported approaches to the determination of meclofenamic acid. As listed in Table 2 , the developed fluorescent sensor demonstrated a lower LOD, comparable or better recoveries, and good precision. The good performances of the developed fluorescent sensor highlight the advantages the sensor offers in simplicity of operation and speed of detection. Furthermore, the proposed method, in contrast to conventional chromatographic techniques, does not require toxic organic solvents as a mobile phase.

This study successfully demonstrates the development and application of a nanocomposite fluorescent probe (N-GQDs@Cit-HA@MIP) for the sensitive detection of meclofenamic acid in dairy products. The sensor outperforms conventional chromatographic methods in sensitivity and speed, offering recoveries of 93.2–99.9 % and excellent reproducibility (RSD ≤ 3.1 %). The integration with smartphone-based detection systems underscores its potential for on-site applications in food safety monitoring. Future work may explore adapting this approach for the detection of other drug residues in diverse food matrices. The limitation of this developed method is that, in the analysis of real samples, it is necessary to ensure that the template has been completely washed out.
